results.title: How to Fix Your Broken RAG System with Agentic RAG | Akshay Bawaliwale posted on the topic | LinkedIn
results.content: 🚨 Your RAG System Is Burning Money. Here's the Fix: Agentic RAG. If your AI retrieves 10 documents for a simple query and still hallucinates on complex ones, your Traditional RAG is fundamentally broken. You’re paying maximum compute for minimum-effort questions. 💸 ❌ The $100K Problem Most Ignore Your current system retrieves identically for every query, can't self-correct, and has zero clue when retrieval is even needed. You are paying for maximum compute on minimum-effort queries. ⚡ Enter: Agentic RAG (The Intelligent Co-Pilot) Stop treating retrieval like a dumb pipe. Use intelligent agents to reason, plan, and adapt, turning retrieval into a smart co-pilot. 🧠 Adaptive Retrieval (Self-RAG): 40% Accuracy Boost. Skips retrieval for simple queries; enables multi-step retrieval for complex [...] Route simple queries to fast paths, reserve heavy retrieval for complexity. 💼 Developer Productivity: Auto-decompose complex queries = less prompt engineering hell. 👨💻 💡 The Brutal Truth: The companies dominating AI have systems that know how to think about which data matters, not just the most data. 🚀 Stop Retrieving Blindly. Start Reasoning Intelligently. That's the difference between AI that responds and AI that solves problems. What's breaking in your RAG pipeline right now? 👇 #AgenticRAG #LLMAgent #AutonomousAgent #AgentArchitecture #MultiAgentSystem #LLMOps #LangChain #LangGraph #QueryPlanning #SelfReflectiveRAG #CorrectiveRAG #QueryDecomposition #ToolUse #FunctionCalling #HybridSearch #SemanticChunking #Reranking #PerformanceOptimization #EnterpriseAI #KnowledgeManagement [...] & 𝗱𝗶𝗳𝗳𝘂𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗺𝗼𝗱𝗲𝗹𝘀 to clean noise and enhance frames —> letting the “Upside-Down” become visible in clear light. So what should you remember? We’re all citizens of a location like Hawkins now. The strange, unseen world of AI is already operating — from security cameras to chatbots, from semantics to vectors. If you want to explore how RAG (#RetrievalAugmentedGeneration), embeddings (#VectorDatabase), multi-agent AI (#AgenticAI) or full-stack AI apps (#FullStackDeveloper) work in simple, real-world language — I’d love to share. Stay curious, stay safe, stay happy. #AI #MachineLearning #DeepLearning #GenAI #LLMs #Embeddings #LangChain #VectorDatabase #FullStack #Dashboard #AIWorkflow

results.title: What is Agentic RAG [+Learn from Human Feedback] | Sprinklr
results.content:
Example: If the human baseline for a ticket is 15 min and agentic RAG completes it in 3 min

→ Efficiency Gain = ((15 – 3) ÷ 15) × 100 = 80% faster

### User satisfaction and NPS

Direct user feedback (CSAT) and Net Promoter Score (NPS) are vital for understanding real-world value and identifying persistent friction points.

Poor scores signal that your agentic RAG is frustrating employees or clients. Think of a support platform that increases ticket escalations instead of resolving them, damaging your brand’s reputation.

If 200 users rate your RAG-powered chatbot, 130 are promoters, 30 are detractors and 40 are passives:

 NPS = (130/200)\100 – (30/200)\100 = 65 – 15 = 50

Related Read: Customer Feedback: How to Collect and Analyze (Latest Guide)

### Response latency [...] ### Response latency

It measures how quickly agentic RAG delivers answers, which is vital for user experience, especially in high-volume or real-time scenarios.

High latency frustrates users. Imagine a procurement team waiting several seconds for contract insights, delaying negotiations and reducing operational agility.

If 500 answers are generated in 400 seconds:

Response Latency = 400/500 = 0.8 seconds per response

## Agentic RAG demands more than adoption; unlock value with the right partner

It’s natural to feel a sense of complexity as agentic RAG becomes the new enterprise standard. But moving from experimentation to true competitive advantage requires more than technical know-how. It calls for the right tools, scalable frameworks, and trusted partnerships. [...] ### Agentic RAG needs a human touch in novel situations

A 2025 joint study by Harvard Business School and MIT highlighted a persistent gap: even advanced agentic AI systems underperform when confronted with edge cases and out-of-distribution data. These include non-standard customer requests, regulatory loopholes, or rare complaint scenarios — the kinds of long-tail situations where retrieval corpora and training data provide little guidance.

Agentic RAG also struggles with novel contexts such as new product launches, unexpected system integrations, or one-off contractual terms, where no historical precedent exists in either model weights or enterprise knowledge bases.

results.title: Agentic RAG: How enterprises are surmounting the limits of ... - Redis
results.content:
### Reliability

Agentic RAG can include numerous different agents, but agentic RAG can sometimes face reliability issues if any of those agents fail or loop. Developers can reduce the likelihood of these issues happening by implementing fast fallback mechanisms. Relevant tools include:

 Cloud provider reliability tooling: This category of tools, including ones from Azure, Google Cloud, and Amazon Web Services, ensures that systems can temporarily stop calls and reroute to backups.
 LangChain: This tool provides built-in iteration limits, retry mechanisms, and structured fallback logic, ensuring that agents won't endlessly loop.
 Apache Airflow: This tool offers centralized orchestration with built-in retries, timeouts, and failover strategies for agent tasks. [...] Memory, coordination, and retrieval latency issues can all make agentic RAG too slow or too cumbersome to be practical. With every agent calling tools, fetching context, and validating results, the real bottleneck tends to be an enterprise's infrastructure, especially under high throughput.

Database

## Now see how this runs in Redis

Power AI apps with real-time context, vector search, and caching.

Get started

## Build the future of retrieval with Redis

Agentic RAG is powerful, but it's not the right fit for every use case. If you're ready to experiment, it offers adaptability, depth, and fine-grained context control. And if you've been using RAG but feel held back by its limits, agentic RAG can help you get to where you thought RAG would take you in the first place. [...] Real-time memory, which can support short- and long-term memory for RAG agents.
 High-speed vector search and hybrid queries to support fast, nuanced queries and retrievals.
 Multi-region availability for geo-distributed agents, which increases scalability.
 Semantic caching to reduce redundant lookups and decrease costs.

Agentic RAG is becoming an increasingly popular topic, but the focus of these conversations tends to be on designing or orchestrating agents. The realities of production show that these systems' failure points tend to be lower in the stack.

results.title: Multimodal Agentic RAG is not possible - even w/ MCP
results.content:
The ArXiv Paper's Finding: The paper provides extensive evidence that the MM-ICL mechanism in current SOTA models is fundamentally flawed. They don't learn; they mimic shallow patterns.

The Inevitable Conclusion: Therefore, the entire MM-RAG system, which relies on this broken mechanism, is itself functionally crippled.

The paper proves that even when you perfect the "R" (Retrieval) by giving the model a perfect, gold-standard document (like a ground-truth rationale), the "G" (Generation) fails to improve because the underlying learning (ICL) is not happening.

------ end of my simple explanation. [...] Learning a specific output format.
Learning a multi-step reasoning process.
Learning how to synthesize information from multiple disparate sources.
Learning how to use a tool based on examples.

All these advanced capabilities are forms of In-Context Learning. If ICL is broken, the model can't learn these things, and RAG is reduced to a simple "find and summarize" tool.

Think of it as a chain reaction:

The Goal: We want to build an advanced MM-RAG system that can reason over retrieved multimodal data.

The Mechanism: This system's effectiveness is entirely dependent on its ability to learn from the retrieved examples and data. This learning mechanism is MM-ICL.

results.title: Agentic RAG Explained
results.content:
_Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) extends traditional RAG by adding an autonomous agent that can reason, plan, and take actions to achieve a goal rather than relying on a single retrieval step. Unlike standard systems that follow a fixed workflow, an agentic approach dynamically breaks down complex tasks, performs multiple searches, uses external tools or APIs, and adapts its strategy as new information emerges. This iterative process improves accuracy, reduces hallucinations, and enables more effective handling of ambiguous or multi-step problems. Key components typically include a decision-making agent, structured retrieval across multiple data sources, memory for context, access to tools, and orchestration to manage execution. Because of these capabilities, agentic RAG is [...] Agentic RAG vs. traditional RAG: Use traditional RAG for simple Q&A. Use agentic RAG when the task requires planning, multistep reasoning, or multiple tools.
   Agentic RAG vs. fine-tuning: Fine-tuning adapts an LLM to a specific style or knowledge domain and is useful for teaching an LLM a new skill. Agentic RAG is about providing an LLM with the ability to reason and use tools. The two approaches are complementary when you use a fine-tuned model as the brain of your agent.
   Agentic RAG vs.prompt engineering: Advanced prompt engineering can lead to impressive behavior from an LLM, but it’s brittle. An agentic framework provides a more structured and reliable way to guide the model’s reasoning process, especially for complex tasks. [...] ## What is agentic RAG?

Agentic RAG is an advanced AI architecture that integrates an autonomous agent with the core components of retrieval-augmented generation. In simple terms, it gives a RAG system the ability to think, plan, and act. Instead of using a single passive retrieval step, an agent actively decides what information it needs, how to get it, and how to use it to achieve a specific goal.
